data.title: Diameter Capital Raises $4.5 Billion in New Commitments for Dislocation Strategy Surpassing Hard Cap
data.description:
Diameter Capital Raises $4.5 Billion in New Commitments for Dislocation Strategy Surpassing Hard Cap

Third Dislocation Fund Received Strong Support from Global Base of Sophisticated Investors

Diameter Capital Partners LP (“Diameter”), an alternative asset manager focused on global credit markets, today announced the successful close of its third dislocation fund, Diameter Dislocation Fund III (the “Fund” or “DDF III”). The Fund and a related vehicle raised $4.5 billion in equity commitments, surpassing the initial hard cap of $4.25 billion. The Fund received strong support from a global base of sophisticated investors, with a third of commitments received from first time investors in Diameter’s Dislocation strategy.

DDF III, which invests in market dislocations through stressed, distressed, and special situations, builds upon Diameter’s track record of identifying and executing investments in industry micro-cycles that occur and create dispersion even in benign macro environments. The Fund seeks to capitalize on Diameter’s deep industry relationships and sector-specific research to source attractive opportunities to serve as a capital solutions provider and generate attractive risk-adjusted returns for investors.

Scott Goodwin and Jonathan Lewinsohn, Co-Founders and Managing Partners of Diameter, said, “Exceeding our initial hard cap for this fundraise demonstrates the trust both new and longstanding investors have in Diameter’s experienced team and disciplined approach. We have already invested meaningful capital to take advantage of the opportunities that exist across today’s dynamic global markets. Looking ahead, we will aim to continue to deploy capital with high-conviction, speed, certainty of execution, and an acute focus on delivering outsized results for our clients."

Diameter continues to scale its investment platform across public and private credit markets with firmwide AUM reaching $25bn. DDF III’s predecessor vehicle, Diameter Dislocation Fund II, closed in 2023 with approximately $2.2 billion of capital commitments and has been in harvest mode since April 2025.

About Diameter

Diameter Capital Partners LP is a global credit manager founded in 2017 by Scott Goodwin and Jonathan Lewinsohn. Diameter seeks to combine trading and research, long and short, to invest across the credit spectrum, from new issue and performing to stressed, distressed, special situations, and equities. Diameter manages $25 billion across its flagship hedge fund, a series of delay draw dislocation funds, and structured credit vehicles. It has offices in New York, London, and Palm Beach.

Jonathan Gasthalter / Amanda Shpiner
Gasthalter & Co
(212) 257-4170
Diameter@gasthalter.com

View source version on businesswire.com: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20251112742246/en/

data.title: How to pick an Obamacare plan now, with premiums likely to soar after government shutdown
data.description:
Andrew Keshner

A promised Senate vote next month on expiring subsidies coincides with a key deadline for people buying Affordable Care Act marketplace plans

The record-breaking government shutdown is over, but the challenges for people buying insurance through the Affordable Care Act look set to continue.

The record-breaking government shutdown has ended, but nearly 24 million people at the heart of the face-off still lack clarity on a major financial decision that they will need to make soon: what sort of health insurance they should buy for next year.

President Donald Trump signed a bill Wednesday night reopening the federal government, soon after the House of Representatives passed the funding bill following the Senate's approval. But the shutdown hinged in part on the fate of expiring tax subsidies for people who bought insurance through Affordable Care Act, or Obamacare, marketplaces.

Under the terms of the deal to end the shutdown, Senate Republicans promised to hold a vote on the future of the ACA tax credits, which were designed to defray out-of-pocket costs for enrollees, by the second week in December. The outcome of the vote on the expiring boost to the tax credit is uncertain, political analysts say.

With the shutdown over, federal workers are poised to return to their jobs and again start receiving paychecks. Travelers are hoping for fewer airport snarls as Thanksgiving nears. Investors are banking on the resumption of important data releases like the monthly jobs report.

Yet independent contractors, small-business owners, early retirees and others who buy health insurance on Affordable Care Act marketplaces have far less certainty. In fact, the pledged Senate vote will coincide with a crucial deadline for ACA enrollees.

To have coverage that starts on Jan. 1, 2026, people need to enroll in or change their plan by Dec. 15, according to Healthcare.gov, the federal government's exchange. Open enrollment on the federal-run marketplace ends on Jan. 15, with coverage starting on Feb. 1 for those who enroll or make changes after Dec. 15.

It's a confusing moment for anyone trying to buy health insurance, said Jason Samel, a New York City-based insurance consultant and founder of NewYorkObamacare.com. "Right now, the best thing we can do is work with what we have."

That may be tough, he acknowledged. Next year's premiums could more than double over those in 2025, according to KFF estimates, with average annual out-of-pocket premium costs potentially rising to $1,904, up from $888. But the cost of skipping insurance could be even greater, Samel said.

The premiums you see are likely what you'll get

For more than a month, Senate Democrats withheld their votes to reopen the government as they pressed for an extension to the pandemic-era enhancement to the tax credit for ACA premiums.

Republicans have balked at the enhanced credit's cost - which would potentially add $350 billion to the federal deficit - and have cited its potential misuse in certain cases. They said they would negotiate the details of the credit once the government reopened.

Now what happens? It's "very unlikely that the enhanced subsidies are extended as is," said Spencer Perlman, managing partner and director of healthcare research at Veda Partners, an investment consulting firm.

At the most, Republicans would likely only agree to a short-term continuation with income caps, he said. They may press for other changes, too. A compromise for a one- or two-year extension of the credits - with reforms pushed by the GOP - is "the most likely outcome."

But there's also a "distinct possibility" the enhanced credit will simply go away entirely, Perlman added. In that case, the original tax credit would apply.

Regardless of what happens in Washington, D.C., health-insurance shoppers' best move may be to plan for no changes. "People should operate under the assumption that the premium payments they see are what they will be paying in 2026," said Emma Wager, senior policy analyst at KFF.

That would make any late-breaking change that reduced premiums the "icing on the cake," according to Samel.

There's still time to control your insurance costs

The original tax credit is a sliding-scale benefit that's structured to pay a portion of an ACA enrollee's costs, depending on income. It stops paying once households hit a certain income level, which is 400% above the federal poverty line.

The pandemic-era enhanced credit further cut out-of-pocket costs, paying more along the sliding scale and also also temporarily lifting the credit's income cutoff, also known as the subsidy cliff.

For 2025, an income of 400% above the federal poverty line for an individual is $62,600. For a family of four, that figure is $128,600.

To determine how much they will pay for ACA marketplace plans, enrollees need to estimate how much income they'll have next year. Avoiding the returning subsidy cliff has become a focal point, said Drew Shockley, co-founder of health-insurance-planning firm Move Health.

That focus is particularly sharp for people who are in early retirement and are still too young to get Medicare coverage, which begins at age 65. "They are getting more aggressive in income management," Shockley said.

Strategies to avoid the cutoff include reducing 2026 retirement-account distributions or delaying claiming Social Security benefits. Another tactic is to speed up Roth conversions in 2025, he said. With a Roth conversion, a person pays taxes to turn pretax IRA savings into aftertax investments.

The money from qualified Roth withdrawals doesn't get tallied as income under the rules for marketplace coverage, according to Healthcare.gov.

Shockley is also seeing clients look beyond the ACA marketplace for health insurance. One alternative is short-term plans. These types of plans are sold in 36 states, according to KFF.

While the premiums may be lower, these plans may have limited benefits and can deny coverage based on pre-existing conditions, KFF researchers noted. Insurance plans sold on ACA exchanges are not allowed to have exclusions for pre-existing conditions.

Despite the potential pitfalls of these plans, Shockley acknowledges that when people are staring at much higher premiums, "you are looking at options." He also sees clients looking into alternatives like faith-based plans that pool money to pay members' healthcare costs.

Some of his clients are holding off on enrollment to see how the rules shape up, Shockley said. He's encouraging them to enroll instead of waiting - but to keep an eye on the news. If Congress makes a late change to the subsidies, he said he would expect lawmakers to add more time for enrollment.

It's also possible to switch plans during the open-enrollment period, experts note.

Some people may be going a little slower. Idaho's state-run exchange began enrollment in mid-October and saw high call volume at first, but that has returned to more typical open-enrollment levels, according to Pat Kelly, executive director and chief executive of Your Health Idaho.

"This, coupled with the website traffic, seems to signal that Idahoans are waiting to see if Congress will take action to extend the enhanced premium tax credits before making a decision about their 2026 coverage," he said.

The danger is in holding off too long. Waiting isn't worth it, as Samel sees it. "First of all, if you need insurance, go get insurance," he said.

Buckle up if there are late-breaking changes - and do this

Suppose lawmakers strike a deal that changes the tax credit and alters the amounts ACA enrollees have to pay. What happens to the people who signed up earlier and budgeted for larger premiums? And what if they want to switch plans in light of any changes?

It's going to be a big headache, Perlman said: "The exchanges will do what they can to adjust to after-the-fact reforms, but it will be very messy and cause a lot of confusion."

Officials at several state-run exchanges told MarketWatch they are preparing their systems and communications efforts in case there is a change, and will attempt to simplify the process for consumers who want to take another look at their coverage.

For example, in Colorado, "our goal is to automatically update premiums for currently enrolled customers without requiring them to reapply, though the final approach will depend on federal guidance and implementation timelines, which are still being finalized," said Kevin Patterson, CEO of Connect for Health Colorado.

"We are following the discussions in Washington, D.C., closely and will act as quickly as possible to apply updated tax credits. We will continue to keep consumers updated on any changes that impact their coverage and the amount of their tax credits," a New York State Department of Health spokesperson said.

People can change plans with no penalty during the open-enrollment period, Wager noted. But this is where it will pay off to have assistance from an agent, broker, accredited ACA navigator or other professional who understands the nuances of the marketplace. "If you are really trying to extract value, that's your best option," she said.

People should be ready in case switching plans winds up being harder than expected. If law changes spur last-minute shifts, "we're going to see a lot of people running to the marketplace at the same time," Shockley said.

"Do I see changing plans going smoothly? Not at all," said Samel. "I don't see any of this going smoothly."

-Andrew Keshner

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  11-13-25 1033ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Mouser Electronics Shows Support for UTA College of Engineering
data.description:
Mouser Electronics Shows Support for UTA College of Engineering

Mouser Electronics, Inc., the New Product Introduction (NPI) leader™ empowering innovation, is proud to support the College of Engineering at The University of Texas at Arlington (UTA), where students are pursuing various fields of engineering to create viable solutions to the most pressing problems of today and the future. The comprehensive engineering program offers 12 baccalaureate, 13 master's, and nine doctoral degree programs. For over 60 years, the College of Engineering has provided highly trained engineers for the workforce in North Texas and beyond.

"Our world is growing more technological every day, making engineering education vital to our business and our shared future. We are very excited to help support UTA's engineering programs," said Kevin Hess, Mouser Senior Vice President of Marketing. "With engineering, anything is possible. These future engineers will be tasked with creating the next technological wonders."

"We are very grateful to Mouser Electronics for their generous support of our engineering programming, events, and other activities," said Peter Crouch, Dean of UTA's College of Engineering. "We strive to deliver an outstanding educational experience, and corporate partnerships play a key role in helping us to achieve that goal."

Mouser, whose global headquarters is located near UTA's college campus, sponsored numerous events in 2025, including the UTA College of Engineering Banquet and the UTA Computer Science and Engineering (CSE) Banquet. The recent CSE luncheon celebrated the 52nd Anniversary and capped off a two-day program featuring presentations by keynote speakers, Q&A sessions with industry experts, and discussion panels with alumni and past department chairs.

Additionally, Mouser team members supported this year's annual UTA Innovation Day in April, where participants submit posters and video presentations online of their design projects. Innovation Day celebrates and showcases the innovative, transformative research and undergraduate capstone projects being done by College of Engineering students. Mouser also sponsored HackUTA 2025 in early October, a 24-hour marathon for students to pitch, design, and develop engineering projects. The event enabled students, regardless of experience, skill, major, or background, to work together in teams to pitch the best solutions possible.

To learn more about Mouser's support of the UTA College of Engineering, visit https://www.mouser.com/uta-engineering/. For more Mouser news and our latest new product introductions, visit https://www.mouser.com/newsroom/.

As a global authorized distributor, Mouser offers the world's widest selection of the newest semiconductors and electronic components — in stock and ready to ship™. Mouser's customers can expect 100% certified, genuine products that are fully traceable from each of its manufacturer partners. To help speed customers' designs, Mouser's website hosts an extensive library of technical resources, including a Technical Resource Center, along with product data sheets, supplier-specific reference designs, application notes, technical design information, engineering tools and other helpful information.

Engineers can stay abreast of today's exciting product, technology and application news through Mouser's complimentary e-newsletter. Mouser's email news and reference subscriptions are customizable to the unique and changing project needs of customers and subscribers. No other distributor gives engineers this much customization and control over the information they receive. Learn about emerging technologies, product trends and more by signing up today at https://sub.info.mouser.com/subscriber/.

About Mouser Electronics

Mouser Electronics is an authorized semiconductor and electronic component distributor focused on New Product Introductions from its leading manufacturer partners. Serving the global electronic design engineer and buyer community, the global distributor's website, mouser.com, is available in multiple languages and currencies and features more than 6.8 million products from over 1,200 manufacturer brands. Mouser offers 28 support locations worldwide to provide best-in-class customer service in local language, currency and time zone. The distributor ships to over 650,000 customers in 223 countries/territories from its 1 million-square-foot, state-of-the-art distribution facilities in the Dallas, Texas, metro area. For more information, visit https://www.mouser.com/.

Trademarks

Mouser and Mouser Electronics are registered trademarks of Mouser Electronics, Inc. All other products, logos, and company names mentioned herein may be trademarks of their respective owners.

For further information, contact:
Kevin Hess, Mouser Electronics
Senior Vice President of Marketing
+1 (817) 804-3833
Kevin.Hess@mouser.com

For press inquiries, contact:
Kelly DeGarmo, Mouser Electronics
Manager, Corporate Communications and Media Relations
+1 (817) 804-7764
Kelly.DeGarmo@mouser.com

data.title: UniCourt DART Receives “Overall LegalTech Data Solution of the Year” from 2025 LegalTech Breakthrough Awards
data.description:
UniCourt DART Receives “Overall LegalTech Data Solution of the Year” from 2025 LegalTech Breakthrough Awards

Prestigious Annual Awards Program Recognizes UniCourt DART as a Product Driving Innovation in the Global Legal Industry

UniCourt is proud to announce that UniCourt DART has been awarded “Overall LegalTech Data Solution of the Year” in the 6th annual LegalTech Breakthrough Awards program conducted by LegalTech Breakthrough, a leading independent market intelligence organization that evaluates and recognizes standout legal technology companies, products, and services around the globe.

This press release features multimedia. View the full release here: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20251113020276/en/

UniCourt DART wins “Overall LegalTech Data Solution of the Year” from 2025 LegalTech Breakthrough Awards

UniCourt DART (Docket Analytics, Research, and Tracking) is a trailblazing all-in-one platform that allows legal professionals to leverage powerful legal analytics, advanced docket research, and seamless case tracking to enhance litigation workflows. With DART, legal professionals can access over 4,000 state and federal courts and over 2 billion dockets and documents (the largest docket database in the industry).

“UniCourt DART has revolutionized legal tech by bringing virtually all court data into one platform in a standardized way. Understanding the litigation landscape, particularly the histories and track records involving key players, is essential to a lawyer’s ability to win cases and business. However, relying on multiple providers, antiquated court portals, and word-of-mouth risks inaccuracies,” said Bryan Vaughn, Managing Director, LegalTech Breakthrough. “UniCourt DART is a game-changing legal tech platform that provides access to litigation data, analytics, and insights across the broadest court coverage in the industry. We’re excited to name UniCourt DART the 2025 recipient of ‘Overall LegalTech Data Solution of the Year!’”

“At UniCourt, our mission is to provide legal professionals with access to the most organized, structured, and comprehensive legal data in the industry, so they can gain the crucial litigation insights that can elevate their practice and business,” said Josh Blandi, CEO of UniCourt. “Winning the 'Overall LegalTech Data Solution of the Year' Breakthrough Award for DART acknowledges our commitment to and achievements towards this mission, and we’re proud to celebrate this recognition!”

DART offers key features and capabilities, such as:

  • delivering actionable insights into the litigation histories of key players based on the most up-to-date litigation data across over 4,000 state and federal courts;
  • allowing users to toggle between natural language searches and granular advanced searches to conduct in-depth docket research; and
  • tracking specific cases to provide reliable real-time docket updates and alerts.

This data solution allows legal professionals to effectively advise clients and craft winning data-backed litigation and business development strategies by showcasing litigation experience. AI-powered data extraction uncovers data buried deep in dockets and documents, with best-in-class standardization and normalization. The resulting data can be accessed not only through UniCourt DART, but also via UniCourt’s API, widgets, or data share and pushed to data warehouses, data lakes, or existing applications using pre-built integrations.

For more information about UniCourt's latest innovations, visit unicourt.com.

About UniCourt

UniCourt is the preeminent legal tech platform that provides access to litigation data, analytics, and insights across the broadest court coverage in the industry. It provides real-time access to over 4,000 state and federal courts across more than 40 states, encompassing over 2 billion dockets and documents, the largest docket database in the industry. UniCourt’s mission is to capture and organize legal data, making it more accessible and useful by applying cutting-edge AI to extract, structure, and normalize the data to provide the next generation of legal services and solutions. Learn more about UniCourt at unicourt.com.

About LegalTech Breakthrough Awards

Part of Tech Breakthrough, a leading market intelligence and recognition platform for global technology innovation and leadership, the LegalTech Breakthrough Awards program is devoted to honoring excellence in legal technologies, services, companies and products. The LegalTech Breakthrough Awards program provides a forum for public recognition around the achievements of LegalTech companies and solutions in categories including Case Management, Client Relations, Data and Analytics, Documentation, Legal Education, Practice Management, eDiscovery, and more. For more information visit LegalTechBreakthrough.com

Tech Breakthrough LLC does not endorse any vendor, product or service depicted in our recognition programs, and does not advise technology users to select only those vendors with award designations. Tech Breakthrough LLC recognition consists of the opinions of the Tech Breakthrough LLC organization and should not be construed as statements of fact. Tech Breakthrough LLC disclaims all warranties, expressed or implied, with respect to this recognition program, including any warranties of merchantability or fitness for a particular purpose.

UniCourt
press@unicourt.com

View source version on businesswire.com: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20251113020276/en/

data.title: ICE Canada Morning Comment: Trying to Retain Increases
data.description:
WINNIPEG, Manitoba--Intercontinental Exchange canola futures were slightly lower on Thursday morning, struggling to hang onto overnight gains.

There's support for canola from increases in the Chicago soy complex and MATIF rapeseed while Malaysian palm oil is relatively steady. Upticks in crude oil were spilling over into the vegetable oils.

Canola was also being affected by pre-report movements on the Chicago Board of Trade as the United States Department of Agriculture will release its supply/demand estimates on Friday.

The January canola contract remained above its 20- and 50-day moving averages, further underpinning the oilseed's values.

As ongoing trade issues with China continued to linger around canola, the federal agriculture minister recently commented some progress is being made to resolve the dispute.

Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada announced it has delayed its monthly supply and demand report from Nov. 19 to Nov. 24.

Statistics Canada is set to release its production update on Dec. 4. The trade expects the canola harvest to well exceed the 20.03 million tons estimated in September.

The Canadian dollar was virtually unchanged on Thursday morning, with the loonie at 71.40 U.S. cents.

Approximately 14,250 contracts were traded by 9:39 EST and prices in Canadian dollars per metric ton were: 

 
           Price      Change 
 
           Price      Change 
Jan       647.60     dn 0.80 
Mar       657.70     dn 0.60 
May       666.90     dn 0.60 
Jul       672.70     dn 0.80

data.title: The longest strike in America has gone on for -2-
data.description:
'Historically, newspaper owners themselves have not been able to survive lengthy strikes, and there are many examples of papers folding after sustained walkouts.'Joshua Freeman, professor of labor history at the City University of New York.

In the early 2010s, the editorial pages of the Post-Gazette and its sister publication, the Toledo Blade, also owned by the Blocks, began turning conservative after decades of being more liberal and pro-labor.

In recent years, the company, which is privately held, has seen its fortunes wane amid broader changes in the media industry as readers, viewers and advertisers shift away from newspapers, cable and local TV.

Late last year, both S&P Global and Moody's downgraded Block Communications' bond ratings, citing high debt and declining revenues.

"In 2025, we believe that earnings will remain flat as broadband revenue growth remains stagnant and is insufficient to offset revenue declines in other segments despite cost-saving initiatives, lower legal and professional-services fees, and fewer losses in publishing," S&P Global wrote.

Since the beginning of the strike, the union has frequently picketed outside the Pittsburgh home of Post-Gazette publisher John Block and outside of events that he and his brother Allan, the chief executive of Block Communications, have attended. The union even hired an airplane to fly a pro-union banner over a Harvard-Yale football game which John Block attended.

Meanwhile, tensions within the Block family have risen over the future of the company. Each brother controls 25% of the company, with family trusts controlling the rest.

The union says John Block has told them he wants to settle the strike, but that other family members have blocked him from doing so.

"Nobody listens to me," Block was quoted as saying in an interview given to the union's news site.

In 2024, Allan Block filed a lawsuit against his brother and several other Block family relatives after he had been pushed out as CEO and chairman. Allan said he had been forced out because he opposed a move by his brother to sell the entire company.

The suit was settled after just a few months, with Allan being reinstated as CEO, although the chairmanship was given to another relative.

In August, the company moved to sell its four local televisions stations in Kentucky, Illinois and Ohio to Gray Media Inc. (GTN) for $80 million.

What's the end game?

The union remains hopeful that this week's appellate ruling will result in its members getting back to work soon.

For the city of Pittsburgh - which has seen a spate of development and tech investment in recent years, as it tries to navigate away from its industrial history and forge a better future than many other Rust Belt cities - the labor strife at its paper of record has proven problematic.

Deputy Mayor Jake Pawlak, who leads the city's Office of Management and Budget, said Pittsburgh's Democratic and pro-union administration had made it a policy not to give interviews or engage directly with the Post-Gazette while the strike continues.

"It raises the question of whether this remains the paper of record for the city of Pittsburgh, and that has only made the media ecosystem here more fragmented," he said. "That has made it a real challenge for this administration to get its message out."

Those who remain on the picket line have struggled to survive. Each has received $500 a week from the strike fund of the Communications Workers of America, the parent union of the Newspaper Guild. They also have received an additional $100 a week from money raised by the local union. Many have been forced to seek side gigs to make ends meet.

Copy editor Erin Hebert said she finished a graduate degree and has worked as a social worker during the dispute, but is keen to get back to working for the paper.

"When we get back in there, I think everyone is ready to just lay their weapons down and get back to work," she said. "There have been people who have had long careers here and were able to build comfortable, middle-class lives, mainly because of the guild. That's all we want."

-Lukas I. Alpert

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  11-13-25 1000ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: The longest strike in America has gone on for 3 years. Is it about to come to an end?
data.description:
By Lukas I. Alpert

'I don't have that much fight left in me:' Most of the journalists at the Pittsburgh Post-Gazette have left, but those still on strike welcome an end to the ordeal

Workers at the Pittsburgh Post-Gazette have been on strike since October 2022, with little signs of an eventual resolution until a significant court ruling this week.

More than three years ago, a group of employees from the Pittsburgh Post-Gazette went on strike - and they have been walking the picket line ever since.

It is currently the longest-running work stoppage in America, and among the most prolonged newspaper strikes in U.S. history. Up until this week, the dispute over sudden changes to employees' healthcare plans and other work rules had dragged through federal court with no end in sight.

But a court ruling on Monday signaled that a resolution may be near.

The strike at the Post-Gazette is a long way off from being the longest in U.S. history; that distinction belongs to the 11-year work stoppage at the Kohler plumbing-supply company that ended in 1965. But the strike at the Pittsburgh newsroom has played out in a way reminiscent of an earlier age in labor history in the U.S., when unions dominated and strikes could turn into bloody conflicts.

In fact, the longest recorded newspaper strike lasted over 10 years at the Wilkes-Barre Times Leader in Pennsylvania, and was marked by violence in which picketers were sprayed with fire hoses by company security. The striking workers also destroyed delivery trucks and beat employees who crossed the picket line. That strike was finally resolved in 1989, and a newspaper created by striking workers, the Citizens Voice, still publishes today.

Union representation in the U.S. has declined significantly since the 1960s, when over 30% of the working population was organized. That figure has since fallen to just under 10% this year, according to U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics.

But one area where unions have seen membership grow in recent years is among media companies, where employees have endured years of layoffs and other cuts as the industry has financially declined.

The dispute in Pittsburgh - in a city with a long pro-labor history, and involving the most prominent media outlet there - has dragged on almost twice as long as the last major newspaper walkout in Detroit, which lasted 19 months before coming to an end in 1997.

This week, the U.S. Third Circuit Court of Appeals ruled in favor of the striking workers in a significant decision that the Newspaper Guild of Pittsburgh says should lead to a resolution. But the Pittsburgh Post-Gazette's management said the ruling, as it stands, could put the paper out of business, and has vowed to appeal.

The number of workers who remain out on the picket line has dwindled to 28, from the 120 who had walked out at the beginning of the strike. Initially, the strike included workers from five unions, but after a series of settlements and buyouts, it's down to just members of the Newspaper Guild.

The number of striking guild members has also shrunk from about 60 when the walkout began, as many had to find other jobs under the financial strain of being out of work for so long.

"I have been on strike for a third of my professional life," said Erin Hebert, a 32-year-old copy editor at the Post-Gazette. "But I believe in journalism, and I think we will win."

"I'm hopeful we can return to a functional newsroom soon," she added. "I don't know how much fight I have left in me."

The paper - owned for over 100 years by Block Communications Inc. - has continued publishing, relying on a staff made up of about 30 employees who declined to go out on strike back in October 2022, and an additional 30 or so reporters hired since then who have crossed the picket line. The paper now only publishes in print two days a week.

At the same time, the striking workers have been publishing stories on their own news site, the Pittsburgh Union Progress.

A long-running dispute

The issues that led to the strike at the Post-Gazette date back to 2017, when the last contract between the newspaper union and the paper expired.

Journalists continued to work without a new contract for the next several years, but relations with management increasingly soured.

In 2018, the paper came under criticism from the union, journalism groups and the then-mayor of Pittsburgh after firing political cartoonist Rob Rodgers for what the mayor said were cartoons deemed too critical of President Donald Trump. The paper's editorial page had been supportive of Trump, but management insisted at the time that politics played no role in Rodgers's dismissal.

Some staffers also accused the paper's publisher of going into stories about Trump late at night and changing the copy to make it more favorable to the president.

'I have been on strike for a third of my professional life.'Erin Hebert, a copy editor at the Pittsburgh Post-Gazette

The following year, the paper's staff was awarded the Pulitzer Prize for breaking news reporting for its coverage of an antisemitic attack on the Tree of Life synagogue in Pittsburgh that left 11 people dead and six wounded.

In 2020, the union complained after management removed a Black reporter from coverage of the Black Lives Matter protests following the killing of George Floyd, saying her social-media posts demonstrated bias. The reporter, Alexis Johnson, filed a discrimination lawsuit against the paper but later dropped it, citing the cost of proceeding.

Things took a turn for the worse that same year when the company unilaterally declared an impasse in negotiations, and said it was abandoning the terms of the expired contract that employees had been working under since 2017. The company abruptly changed several work rules and switched employees to the healthcare plan for Block Communications, which cost workers significantly more, rather than the one agreed to under the previous union contract, the union said.

In October 2022, the Newspaper Guild plus four other unions, including the Teamsters and others representing printing-press workers, walked off the job and demanded a return to the terms of the 2017 contract.

A spokeswoman for the Post-Gazette said the paper and Block Communications had no comment on the union's allegations, and declined to discuss what the company viewed as an equitable solution to the labor dispute.

The matter has since been bogged down in court. In 2023, the National Labor Relations Board found that the Post-Gazette had violated federal labor laws by not bargaining in good faith. The paper appealed the decision, and the case has been bouncing around in federal appeals court since.

In 2024, the Teamsters reached a settlement in which its remaining members agreed to take a buyout and the union local was dissolved. Soon after, the Post-Gazette announced it was closing its printing press. Early in 2025, three other unions representing production and advertising workers similarly agreed to take buyouts.

Monday's ruling, by a three-judge panel on the Third Circuit Court of Appeals in Philadelphia, reaffirmed that the Post-Gazette had bargained in bad faith and ordered the paper to restore the terms of the 2017 contract.

The ruling also requires the paper to reimburse employees the difference in the cost of the healthcare plans they were forced into in 2020, plus any other costs for losses such as vacation-day accruals that were scrapped when the company abandoned the old contract.

The union said it viewed this as a significant step toward resolving the strike and that its members were prepared to get back to work. But the Post-Gazette said the costs associated with the court's ruling could be devastating and that it planned to appeal the decision.

"If allowed to stand, this decision will likely force the closure of the Post-Gazette," the paper said in a statement.

The Post-Gazette has two weeks from the ruling to file an appeal for the case to be heard by a full panel of 14 judges at the Third Circuit Court of Appeals. That panel would be expected to rule within two months, and if the paper was unhappy with the outcome, they could then attempt an appeal to the U.S. Supreme Court.

Legal experts say that it is unusual for a media company's owner to take a labor dispute this far.

"Historically, newspaper owners themselves have not been able to survive lengthy strikes, and there are many examples of papers folding after sustained walkouts," said Joshua Freeman, a professor of labor history at the City University of New York. "That the Post-Gazette has hung on this long is even more surprising given the poor state of the newspaper industry overall."

He said the length of the strike was likely driven, at least in part, by the paper being family-owned.

"Many of the longest strikes involved family businesses where it becomes more personal, rather than with a corporate-owned entity where more straightforward business decisions tend to drive things," Freeman said.

All in the family

At the center of the dispute has been the Block family.

The paper first came into their possession in 1927, when Paul Block, a politically connected New York advertising tycoon, bought the Pittsburgh Gazette Times from his friend William Randolph Hearst, as well as the Pittsburgh Post, and merged the two publications.

He acquired several other newspapers in the region, forming Block Communications. He died in 1941 and was succeeded by his son, William Block, and then his grandsons, twin brothers Allan and John R. Block. Over time, the family added several local television stations in the region to its holdings, and later added a broadband internet and cable company to the mix.

Over the years, as newspapers' fortunes diminished, the Post-Gazette became the dominant news source in Pittsburgh - absorbing its biggest rival, the Pittsburgh Press, in the early 1990s following a devastating strike there.

(MORE TO FOLLOW) Dow Jones Newswires

11-13-25 1000ET

data.title: Press Club Statement On Chinese Court Denial of Appeal in Dong Yuyu Case
data.description:
Press Club Statement On Chinese Court Denial of Appeal in Dong Yuyu Case

Press Club Statement On Chinese Court Denial of Appeal in Dong Yuyu Case

PR Newswire

WASHINGTON, Nov. 13, 2025

WASHINGTON, Nov. 13, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- The following statement is from Mike Balsamo, president of the National Press Club.

"The National Press Club sharply condemns the decision by the Beijing High Court to uphold the seven-year sentence against journalist Dong Yuyu. By refusing to address the central issues raised on appeal, the court has ensured that Dong will remain behind bars for years to come — not for wrongdoing, but for the ordinary professional work that journalists around the world carry out every day.

"This ruling is a deep injustice. Dong's alleged "offense" consists of routine conversations, meetings, and reporting — the very activities that underpin a free press and an informed public. When a journalist is imprisoned for engaging in normal professional exchanges, it signals a profound retreat from transparency and the rule of law.

"Keeping a journalist confined for years because of his work is indefensible. Great nations do not fear reporters — they protect them. Dong should be released on time served and allowed to return to his family.

"We are also deeply concerned that Dong's family, who were present in the courtroom, were not permitted to speak with him. Their continued separation highlights the human cost of this case and the urgent need for relief." 

Founded in 1908, the National Press Club is the world's leading professional organization for journalists. With 2,500 members, the Club, through its Press Freedom Center, is a leading voice for a free press in the U.S. and worldwide.

CONTACT: Bill McCarren, 202-662-7534 for the Press Freedom Center at the National Press Club or media@press.org

View original content to download multimedia:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/press-club-statement-on-chinese-court-denial-of-appeal-in-dong-yuyu-case-302614638.html

SOURCE National Press Club

data.title: Clairity Raises $43 Million in Series B to Launch First FDA-Authorized AI Platform for Breast Cancer Risk Prediction
data.description:
Clairity Raises $43 Million in Series B to Launch First FDA-Authorized AI Platform for Breast Cancer Risk Prediction

With backing from leading healthcare investors, Clairity accelerates its mission to predict and prevent breast cancer and expand into cardiovascular disease

Clairity, Inc., an AI-driven precision health company pioneering image-based risk prediction, today announced the closing of a $43 million Series B financing. The funds will be used to commercialize the company’s product, Clairity Breast, the first FDA-authorized artificial intelligence platform that predicts a woman’s five-year risk of breast cancer directly from a routine mammogram, and to advance development of Clairity Breast 3D and Clairity Heart, Clairity’s next offering focused on predicting cardiovascular risk from the same routine imaging exam.

The round was led by ACE Global Equity and Santé Ventures, with continued participation from the Breast Cancer Research Foundation (BCRF) and several new investors. The strong commitment from the syndicate reflects broad confidence in Clairity’s mission to transform how breast cancer is found, managed, and ultimately prevented, and in the power of the company’s AI platform to extend into other areas of population health.

The new funding will allow Clairity to accelerate U.S. commercialization, expand partnerships with leading imaging centers and health systems, and drive continued development of Clairity’s broader predictive health platform.

Following the successful FDA authorization of Clairity Breast and its initial rollout to marquee health systems, the company is expanding its pipeline to include new AI-based predictive offerings for other major diseases, leveraging the same deep learning framework on which Clairity Breast was built. The Series B financing will also support reimbursement initiatives and partnerships aimed at making Clairity Breast accessible across community imaging centers nationwide.

“For decades, we have relied on mammography to detect cancer only after it is visible,” said Connie Lehman, MD, PhD, Founder of Clairity. “With Clairity Breast, we can now use the same images to predict who may be at risk over the next five years, supporting earlier intervention, fewer late-stage diagnoses, and more lives saved. This is the future of breast cancer care: predictive, personalized, and preventive.”

“Our fundraising is about more than capital; it’s about momentum,” said Jeff Luber, Clairity’s President and CEO. “With FDA authorization in hand and commercial partnerships underway, Clairity Breast is poised to redefine how the world approaches breast cancer. We’re proud to stand at the forefront of a paradigm shift—from detection to prevention—and to deliver technology that empowers women and their doctors to predict risk to support action before cancer starts.”

“Clairity represents a rare combination of scientific rigor, regulatory validation, and transformative clinical impact,” said Gabriella Antici, Partner and Chief Investment Officer, ACE Global Equity. “Predictive imaging is the next frontier in oncology, and Clairity Breast is the first real-world embodiment of that promise.”

“BCRF has long believed that prevention is the ultimate cure,” said Donna McKay, President and CEO, Breast Cancer Research Foundation. “Clairity’s technology embodies that vision, turning existing imaging infrastructure into a proactive tool for risk stratification toward earlier interventions that can save lives. We’re proud to continue supporting their work as they bring this innovation to women everywhere.”

“Santé is proud to support Clairity in advancing a new era of predictive breast health,” said Kevin Lalande, Founding Managing Director and Chief Investment Officer of Santé Ventures. “By pairing clinically validated AI with scalable access channels, Clairity exemplifies the type of innovation that can help drive better outcomes for patients and greater cost efficiency across the healthcare system.”

“Clairity’s work represents exactly the kind of innovation we need in healthcare, where data and AI move us from reacting to disease to preventing it,” said Joe Kiani, Founder and Executive Chairman of Willow Laboratories, and Founder of global medical technology company, Masimo (NASDAQ: MASI). “By transforming routine imaging into a window of prediction, Clairity empowers physicians and patients alike to act earlier, which should lead to better outcomes.” Mr. Kiani joins Clairity as investor and board member.

TCB Capital Advisors (“TCB”) served as exclusive financial advisor to Clairity with respect to the Series B financing.

About Clairity, Inc.

Clairity is an AI-powered precision health company transforming medical imaging into a predictive tool for early risk assessment. Its flagship product, Clairity Breast, is the first FDA-cleared platform to estimate a woman’s five-year breast cancer risk directly from a routine mammogram. By shifting care from detection to prevention, Clairity empowers health systems, radiologists, and women to act earlier, save lives, and lower the overall cost of cancer care.

For more information, visit www.clairity.com.

Investor Contact: Jenna Barton; jbarton@clairity.com

View source version on businesswire.com: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20251113374784/en/
